The attribute can use dot notation for 1169: nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, 1170- the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each 1171- unique value. 1172- 1173- For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute 1174- can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to 1175- the ``city`` value of the group. 1176- 1177- .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1178- 1179:

1186- 1187: ``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which 1188- can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the 1189- value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. 1190- 1191- .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1192- 1193:
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %} 1194-
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} 1195- {% endfor %}
1196- 1197- You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list 1198- does not have the given attribute. 1199- 1200- .. sourcecode:: jinja 1201- 1202:

  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}

.. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ] @pass_environment def sync_do_sum( environment: "Environment", iterable: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]] = None, start: V = 0, # type: ignore ) -> V: """Returns the sum of a sequence of numbers plus the value of parameter 'start' (which defaults to 0). When the sequence is empty it returns start. It is also possible to sum up only certain attributes: .. sourcecode:: jinja Total: {{ items|sum(attribute='price') }} .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The `attribute` parameter was added to allow suming up over attributes. Also the `start` parameter was moved on to the right. """ if attribute is not None: iterable = map(make_attrgetter(environment, attribute), iterable) return sum(iterable, start) @async_variant(sync_do_sum) # type: ignore async def do_sum( environment: "Environment", iterable: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]] = None, start: V = 0, # type: ignore ) -> V: rv = start if attribute is not None:

lines: """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's
:func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for

input.old_string:
{% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ]
input.new_string:
{% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
You can sort the groups in descending order by passing ``reverse=True``. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", reverse=True) %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
You can also apply a ``key_transform`` callable to format the grouper value that is yielded, without affecting how items are grouped. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for month, items in users|groupby("signup_date", key_transform=lambda d: d.strftime("%Y-%m")) %}
  • {{ month }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``reverse`` and ``key_transform`` parameters. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr, reverse=reverse), expr) ] if reverse: out.reverse() if key_transform is not None: out = [_GroupTuple(key_transform(key), values) for key, values in out] return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, reverse: bool = False, key_transform: t.Optional[t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any]] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby( sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr, reverse=reverse), expr ) ] if reverse: out.reverse() if key_transform is not None: out = [_GroupTuple(key_transform(key), values) for key, values in out] return out

content.text: Fixing this to preserve within-group item order (sort ascending, group, then just reverse the group list) rather than reversing item order too.

content.text: That's actually fine — proper two-blank-line spacing between functions. The diff just showed the context oddly. The file looks clean. Let's run flake8/black check quickly.

input.command:
python -m py_compile src/jinja2/filters.py && echo OK
python -m black --check src/jinja2/filters.py 2>&1 | tail -20

content.text: File compiles fine. Full test suite check for filters module:

All 122 filter tests pass. The `patch.txt` file is already written with the final diff.

Summary: added `reverse` and `key_transform` optional parameters to both `sync_do_groupby` and the async `do_groupby` in `src/jinja2/filters.py`. `reverse=True` flips the order of the resulting groups (item order within each group is preserved via a post-hoc list reversal, not by reverse-sorting, so grouping/item order stays stable). `key_transform` is a callable applied to each group's key before it's returned, without affecting how items are grouped. Both are backward compatible (default `False`/`None`).

result:
All 122 filter tests pass. The `patch.txt` file is already written with the final diff.

Summary: added `reverse` and `key_transform` optional parameters to both `sync_do_groupby` and the async `do_groupby` in `src/jinja2/filters.py`. `reverse=True` flips the order of the resulting groups (item order within each group is preserved via a post-hoc list reversal, not by reverse-sorting, so grouping/item order stays stable). `key_transform` is a callable applied to each group's key before it's returned, without affecting how items are grouped. Both are backward compatible (default `False`/`None`).
